title: Krylov complexity has it all
abstract: This paper establishes that Krylov complexity contains the entire information about the dynamics of a quantum operator, extending the list of equivalent quantities that can serve this purpose, such as the Lanczos coefficients, the return amplitude, and the spectral density. To demonstrate this equivalence, an explicit recursive algorithm is constructed to calculate Lanczos coefficients from the Taylor expansion of the Krylov complexity around $t=0$. Furthermore, the paper discusses the distinction between Krylov and spread complexity, clarifying why a similar recursive algorithm cannot exist for the latter without additional dynamical input. These results provide a ``proof of principle'' for using Krylov complexity as a complete characterization of operator evolution in quantum systems.
summary: This paper demonstrates that Krylov complexity encodes all information about the dynamics of a quantum operator. It provides a specific mathematical procedure to reconstruct the underlying Lanczos coefficients from the time-evolution of the complexity. This establishes Krylov complexity as a complete diagnostic tool for operator growth.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether Krylov complexity contains the complete information required to reconstruct the full dynamics of a quantum operator.
main_result: The author proves that Krylov complexity is a complete characterization of operator evolution and provides a recursive algorithm to extract Lanczos coefficients from its Taylor expansion.
method: The study employs Taylor expansion analysis of the Krylov complexity and its moments, combined with the Lanczos algorithm and properties of orthogonal polynomials.
model_or_system: The dynamics are modeled using a quantum operator evolving under a Liouvillian, which is mapped onto a one-dimensional semi-infinite chain (Krylov basis) described by a hopping model.
key_observables: Krylov complexity K(t), second spread complexity K2(t), return amplitude, and spectral density.
important_parameters: Lanczos coefficients (b_n, Delta_n) and diagonal terms (a_n) representing the Liouvillian action.
main_assumptions: The reconstruction algorithm assumes that the input Taylor coefficients represent a valid, physically consistent Krylov complexity.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the equivalence of Krylov complexity to other dynamical quantities, presents a recursive algorithm for coefficient extraction, and concludes by comparing Krylov complexity to spread complexity.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a fundamental 'proof of principle' for using a single complexity measure to fully characterize the evolution of quantum operators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
